title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Dubu Jorim Recipe: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make authentic Dubu Jorim, a delicious spicy Korean braised tofu dish. This vegetarian recipe is easy, flavorful, and perfect for any meal. Get the full recipe!
longTailKeywords: Dubu Jorim spicy braised tofu recipe
seo.h1: Authentic Dubu Jorim: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu Recipe
h2Headings: What is Dubu Jorim (Spicy Braised Tofu)?
Tips for Perfect Spicy Braised Tofu
Variations & Substitutions for This Korean Tofu Dish
seo.imageAlt: A bowl of freshly made Dubu Jorim, spicy Korean braised tofu, garnished with green onions and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy this flavorful and easy-to-make Dubu Jorim, a classic Korean vegetarian dish that's perfect for a weeknight meal!

description: Dubu Jorim is a beloved Korean vegetarian dish featuring pan-fried tofu simmered in a savory, spicy, and slightly sweet gochujang-based sauce. This comforting and flavorful braised tofu offers a delightful texture and a robust depth of taste that perfectly complements a bowl of warm rice. It's a quick and healthy meal that brings the authentic flavors of Korea to your table.

instructions.title: Prepare the Tofu
instructions.text: Slice each block of drained tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles. Lay them on a paper towel-lined plate, cover with more paper towels, and place a heavy object on top for at least 20 minutes to press out excess water. This helps the tofu absorb flavor and become firm.

instructions.title: Pan-Fry the Tofu
instructions.text: Heat vegetable oil in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Carefully add the pressed tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-4 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. Remove the fried tofu and set aside on a plate.

instructions.title: Prepare the Braising Sauce
instructions.text: In a medium bowl, whisk together soy sauce, gochujang, gochugaru, minced garlic, sesame oil, rice vinegar, sugar, and water or vegetable broth until well combined and smooth.

instructions.title: Simmer the Sauce
instructions.text: Pour the prepared braising sauce into the same pan (no need to clean it) and bring it to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Allow the sauce to cook for 2-3 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it slightly thickens and the flavors meld.

instructions.title: Braise the Tofu
instructions.text: Carefully add the pan-fried tofu slices back into the simmering sauce. Arrange them in a single layer if possible, ensuring each piece is coated. Reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let it braise for 15-20 minutes, or until the tofu has absorbed the sauce and it has thickened to your desired consistency. Gently flip the tofu halfway through the braising time to ensure even coating.

instructions.title: Garnish and Serve
instructions.text: Once the Dubu Jorim is ready, remove it from heat. Transfer the braised tofu and sauce to a serving dish. Garnish generously with chopped green onions and toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately with warm rice and other Korean side dishes.

tips: For extra-firm tofu, pressing out excess water is crucial to prevent crumbling and allow better flavor absorption during braising.
Adjust the amount of gochugaru (Korean chili flakes) to control the spice level; add more for extra heat or less for a milder dish.
Dubu Jorim tastes even better the next day as the flavors deepen. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days.
article:
Discover the heart of Korean home cooking with Dubu Jorim, a beloved vegetarian dish that tantalizes the taste buds with its rich, spicy, and savory flavors. If you're looking to explore the vibrant world of Korean cuisine or simply seeking a delicious, wholesome plant-based meal, this Spicy Braised Tofu is an absolute must-try. Easy to make and incredibly satisfying, Dubu Jorim transforms humble tofu into a culinary masterpiece, proving that vegetarian dishes can be just as bold and flavorful as any meat-centric meal. It's not just food; it's a comforting embrace, a testament to the art of Korean braising that will leave you craving more.

## What is Dubu Jorim (Spicy Braised Tofu)?
Dubu Jorim, literally translating to "braised tofu," is a classic Korean banchan (side dish) or light main course made by pan-frying slices of firm tofu until golden, then simmering them in a deeply flavorful, spicy sauce. The sauce is typically a harmonious blend of gochugaru (Korean chili flakes), soy sauce, garlic, sesame oil, and a touch of sweetness, often from sugar or corn syrup. This simmering process allows the tofu to fully absorb the rich flavors of the braising liquid, resulting in a dish that is tender on the inside, slightly crisp on the outside, and bursting with a complex sweet, savory, and spicy taste.

Known for its vibrant red hue and intoxicating aroma, Dubu Jorim is a staple in Korean households and a favorite among those who appreciate robust flavors. It’s a perfect example of how simple, everyday ingredients can be elevated into something extraordinary through traditional cooking techniques. Beyond its deliciousness, it's also a highly nutritious dish, packed with protein from the tofu, making it an excellent choice for a healthy and satisfying meal.

## Ingredients for Dubu Jorim
To create authentic and flavorful Dubu Jorim, selecting the right ingredients is key. Here’s what you’ll need:

For the Tofu:
*   **Firm or Extra-Firm Tofu:** Approximately 14-16 oz (400-450g) block. This type holds its shape best during pan-frying and braising.
*   **Cooking Oil:** A neutral oil like vegetable, canola, or grapeseed oil for pan-frying.

For the Spicy Braising Sauce:
*   **Gochugaru (Korean Chili Flakes):** 2-3 tablespoons, adjust to your spice preference. This is crucial for both heat and the signature red color.
*   **Soy Sauce:** 3 tablespoons. Use a good quality Korean soy sauce for best results.
*   **Garlic:** 3-4 cloves, minced.
*   **Sesame Oil:** 1 tablespoon. Adds a distinct nutty aroma and flavor.
*   **Sugar or Corn Syrup:** 1-2 tablespoons, to balance the spice and saltiness.
*   **Water or Vegetable Broth:** 1/2 to 3/4 cup, for the braising liquid.
*   **Optional:** 1 teaspoon gochujang (Korean chili paste) for deeper umami and thicker sauce, 1 teaspoon rice vinegar for a slight tang, or 1 tablespoon mirin for added sweetness and depth.

For Garnish:
*   **Green Onions (Scallions):** 2 stalks, chopped, for freshness and color.
*   **Toasted Sesame Seeds:** 1 teaspoon, for a final flourish and nutty crunch.

Having these ingredients ready will ensure a smooth and enjoyable cooking process for your Dubu Jorim.

## Step-by-Step Dubu Jorim Instructions
Making Dubu Jorim is a straightforward process that yields incredibly rewarding results. Follow these steps for a perfect batch:

1.  **Prepare the Tofu:**
    *   Drain the tofu block and press it to remove excess water. You can do this by wrapping it in paper towels or a clean kitchen towel, placing it on a plate, and weighing it down with something heavy (like a cast-iron skillet or a few cans) for at least 20-30 minutes. This step is crucial for firm tofu that won't crumble and will absorb flavors better.
    *   Once pressed, slice the tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles or squares.
    *   Heat 2-3 tablespoons of cooking oil in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Carefully place the tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan.
    *   Pan-fry the tofu for 3-5 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. Remove the fried tofu from the pan and set aside.

2.  **Prepare the Braising Sauce:**
    *   In a medium bowl, combine the gochugaru, soy sauce, minced garlic, sesame oil, sugar (or corn syrup), and any optional ingredients like gochujang, rice vinegar, or mirin. Mix well until all ingredients are thoroughly combined into a smooth paste.

3.  **Braise the Tofu:**
    *   In the same pan (or a clean one if preferred), arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer.
    *   Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu.
    *   Add the water or vegetable broth to the pan. The liquid should cover about half to two-thirds of the tofu.
    *   Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Once simmering, reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let it braise for 10-15 minutes.
    *   Periodically, gently spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure even flavor distribution. The sauce will thicken as it reduces.
    *   Cook until the sauce has thickened to your desired consistency and the tofu has absorbed the rich flavors.

4.  **Garnish and Serve:**
    *   Once cooked, turn off the heat. Garnish the Dubu Jorim generously with chopped green onions and toasted sesame seeds.
    *   Serve hot and enjoy!

## Tips for Perfect Spicy Braised Tofu
Achieving the perfect Dubu Jorim is all about a few key techniques and considerations.

*   **Pressing is Non-Negotiable:** Don't skip pressing the tofu. This step removes excess water, preventing soggy tofu, allowing it to crisp up beautifully when pan-fried, and enabling it to better absorb the flavorful braising sauce.
*   **Golden Brown Tofu:** Ensure your tofu is nicely browned on both sides before braising. This creates a slightly crispy exterior that holds its shape and adds a pleasant textural contrast to the soft interior once braised.
*   **Adjust Spice to Your Liking:** The amount of gochugaru is entirely up to your personal preference. Start with 2 tablespoons for a moderate spice level and add more if you prefer a fiery kick. You can also add a teaspoon of gochujang for a deeper, more rounded heat.
*   **Gentle Simmer:** Braise the tofu over low heat. A gentle simmer prevents the tofu from becoming tough and allows it to slowly absorb the sauce without breaking apart.
*   **Don't Over-Reduce the Sauce:** Keep an eye on the sauce while braising. You want it to thicken enough to coat the tofu, but not to completely dry out. A little extra sauce is perfect for mixing with rice.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Always taste the sauce before and during braising. Adjust the sweetness, saltiness, and spice as needed. A dash more soy sauce, a pinch of sugar, or a sprinkle of gochugaru can make all the difference.

## Serving Suggestions for Dubu Jorim
Dubu Jorim is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in many ways. Traditionally, it's served as a banchan (side dish) alongside a bowl of steaming hot rice and other Korean dishes. The rich, spicy sauce is fantastic when mixed directly into rice, creating a flavorful bite every time.

Beyond a simple side, you can also serve it as a light vegetarian main course with a simple clear soup and a variety of other banchan like kimchi, seasoned spinach (sigeumchi namul), or bean sprout salad (kongnamul muchim). It pairs wonderfully with virtually any Korean meal, adding a burst of flavor and a healthy protein boost. Don't hesitate to enjoy it fresh, as the flavors are most vibrant right after cooking, though it also makes for excellent leftovers.

## Variations & Substitutions for This Korean Tofu Dish
While the classic Dubu Jorim is perfect as is, there are many ways to customize this Korean tofu dish to suit your taste or available ingredients.

*   **Add Vegetables:** Incorporate sliced onions, zucchini, mushrooms, or bell peppers into the braising process. Add them a few minutes after the tofu has started simmering so they cook through but retain some texture.
*   **Less Spicy Version:** If you're sensitive to heat, significantly reduce the amount of gochugaru. You can also add a bit more sugar or mirin to sweeten the sauce and balance the spice.
*   **Enhanced Umami:** For an extra layer of savory depth, consider adding a teaspoon of mushroom powder, a dash of vegetarian "fish" sauce, or even a small spoonful of doenjang (Korean soybean paste) to the sauce.
*   **Gluten-Free Option:** Simply substitute regular soy sauce with tamari to make this dish entirely gluten-free.
*   **Different Tofu Textures:** While firm or extra-firm tofu is recommended, you could try using medium-firm tofu for a softer texture, though it might be more prone to breaking.
*   **Sweetness Adjustment:** Experiment with different sweeteners like brown sugar, maple syrup, or agave nectar instead of white sugar or corn syrup for subtle flavor variations.

Dubu Jorim is more than just a dish; it's an experience of Korean culinary warmth and flavor. Its spicy, savory, and slightly sweet profile makes it an irresistible addition to any meal, whether you're a seasoned Korean food enthusiast or just beginning your culinary journey. This wholesome and comforting braised tofu is a testament to the simple yet profound deliciousness that can be achieved with plant-based ingredients. So, gather your ingredients, follow these steps, and bring the authentic taste of Korea into your kitchen with a delightful batch of Dubu Jorim.
